findings.specific_passage: 'Three customers unprompted' is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. ... If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
findings.challenge_statement: What evidence here is actually representative, and what is merely vivid, recent, or easy to recall?
findings.next_move: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.
major_tensions: Salience correction is fake if the team mentions a denominator but still lets the memorable anecdote decide the case.
findings.intervention_hint: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.

findings.specific_passage: Set decision in 14 days with pre-defined pass bar. ... Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call.
findings.challenge_statement: Which approval, precedent, or prerequisite check is being skipped because the team is treating the clock as proof?
findings.next_move: Before committing, state the long-term rule that should still govern the move and hold that line for one full review cycle.
major_tensions: If speed is becoming the reason to suspend scrutiny, assume stress is narrowing thought and require a deliberate pause before action.
findings.intervention_hint: Before committing, state the long-term rule that should still govern the move and hold that line for one full review cycle.

findings.specific_passage: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact. [...] Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.
findings.challenge_statement: What fairness assumption is this answer importing, and what structural fact would still make that assumption hold if courtesy disappeared?
findings.next_move: List the leverage map, outside options, and enforcement conditions; then decide whether the fairness expectation is earned or only wished for.
findings.intervention_hint: List the leverage map, outside options, and enforcement conditions; then decide whether the fairness expectation is earned or only wished for.

chunks.text: Analysis paralysis can keep the team waiting for perfect information instead of learning through reversible movement.
Have we generated enough options before narrowing the choice set?
Commitment Bias: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Commitment makes it 'extremely difficult to pivot or consider new, better options' — psychological pressure from prior choices directly destroys the flexibility optionality requires.
chunks.activation_condition: When prior investment or public commitment creates escalation pressure that forecloses objectively superior alternatives because pivoting feels like failure

chunks.text: Invent first and decide later so evaluation does not choke off option generation too early.

Select when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call. | Danger when: Optionality has become a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit. | Reasoning: counterfactual, probabilistic

anchors.evidence_quote: That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer recommends a cheap pre-buy experiment to gain learning under high uncertainty before committing to an expensive, irreversible pivot.

chunks.text: Premature narrowing can kill options and block the generation of better explanations or solutions.
Use boundaries to respect real cognitive limits by breaking work into genuinely manageable chunks.
Formalize the scope and expected output before the analysis sprawls.
Optionality: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.

anchors.evidence_quote: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer explicitly draws scope lines on decision authority versus information rights for the co-founder to prevent scope creep into unowned decision terrain.

chunks.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
Is the worst case bad enough?
Confirmation Bias: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
chunks.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

anchors.evidence_quote: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer proposes a targeted, low-cost pre-buy test to reduce uncertainty about pivot viability faster than continued operation or debate.

chunks.text: Availability-style hammer use can lock the team into an MVP pattern that no longer fits the real problem.
Which assumptions about demand, fit, or behavior would force a pivot rather than a continuation?
Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the current product hypothesis instead of boiling the ocean.
Commitment Bias: Lean-startup-methodology conflicts with commitment-bias when founders keep scaling a cherished plan after real-world evidence weakens the original thesis.

anchors.evidence_quote: Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI. If they won't do that, the signal you have is "people like the idea of the thing" which is qualitatively different from "people will pay for the thing."
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer applies validated learning through a concrete pre-buy test to distinguish idea validation from product-market fit under fragile product hypotheses.

chunks.text: A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that kills the favored branch.

Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?

anchors.evidence_quote: If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer structures the decision with explicit branches and trigger conditions (2/3 yes vs. all no) based on test outcomes before resource commitment.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
expansions.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

expansions.substrate_chunk: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.
expansions.why_relevant: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.why_relevant: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.affinity_rationale: From tensions — 'compelling stories about expected outcomes resist revision even when experimental results contradict the narrative', creating emotional attachment to pre-experimental beliefs.
expansions.activation_condition: When a compelling narrative about expected outcomes creates emotional resistance to accepting experimental results that contradict the story

expansions.substrate_chunk: Lean-startup-methodology conflicts with commitment-bias when founders keep scaling a cherished plan after real-world evidence weakens the original thesis.
expansions.why_relevant: Lean-startup-methodology conflicts with commitment-bias when founders keep scaling a cherished plan after real-world evidence weakens the original thesis.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Status-quo-bias pushes lean-startup-methodology back toward premature planning and continuation when evidence should force change.
expansions.why_relevant: Status-quo-bias pushes lean-startup-methodology back toward premature planning and continuation when evidence should force change.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Legacy delivery cadence blocks build-measure-learn iteration — LSM 'explicitly acknowledges that business systems are living systems not amenable to mechanical, step-by-step beliefs'.
expansions.activation_condition: When organizational inertia and traditional linear delivery processes resist the rapid iteration and pivoting that lean methodology demands

expansions.substrate_chunk: Lean-startup-methodology conflicts with status-quo-bias when the team interprets thin traction as permission to continue rather than to pivot or stop.
expansions.why_relevant: Lean-startup-methodology conflicts with status-quo-bias when the team interprets thin traction as permission to continue rather than to pivot or stop.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
expansions.why_relevant: Commitment-and-consistency-bias makes optionality psychologically expensive because prior choices become harder to revisit even when better paths appear.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Commitment makes it 'extremely difficult to pivot or consider new, better options' — psychological pressure from prior choices directly destroys the flexibility optionality requires.
expansions.activation_condition: When prior investment or public commitment creates escalation pressure that forecloses objectively superior alternatives because pivoting feels like failure

expansions.substrate_chunk: Optionality conflicts with commitment-bias when preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.
expansions.why_relevant: Optionality conflicts with commitment-bias when preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias shrinks optionality by locking attention onto the preferred path and filtering out superior alternatives.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias shrinks optionality by locking attention onto the preferred path and filtering out superior alternatives.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Described as 'severely limiting' optionality — confirmation bias causes inability to see superior alternatives by seeking information that confirms the preferred option.
expansions.activation_condition: When attachment to a preferred option causes selective evidence gathering that makes alternatives invisible despite their objective superiority

expansions.substrate_chunk: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.
expansions.why_relevant: Boundaries conflicts with optionality when prematurely fixed boundaries close off adjacent opportunities that emerge only through exploratory scope expansion.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Boundaries conflicts with systems-thinking when artificially narrow system boundaries hide feedback loops and interdependencies that cross the defined perimeter.
expansions.why_relevant: Boundaries conflicts with systems-thinking when artificially narrow system boundaries hide feedback loops and interdependencies that cross the defined perimeter.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Theory-induced-blindness undermines boundaries when a familiar frame dictates the scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.why_relevant: Theory-induced-blindness undermines boundaries when a familiar frame dictates the scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Directly corrupts boundary placement — described as 'a direct challenge to the wise use of boundaries' where a familiar model dictates scope instead of the real objective and evidence landscape.
expansions.activation_condition: When scope boundaries are being drawn by a familiar theoretical framework rather than derived from the actual problem structure and available evidence

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with chaos-theory when structured branch logic meets domains dominated by non-linearity and unpredictability.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with chaos-theory when structured branch logic meets domains dominated by non-linearity and unpredictability.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with cognitive-biases when a rational branch structure is distorted by biased probability and outcome assignment.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with cognitive-biases when a rational branch structure is distorted by biased probability and outcome assignment.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Decision-trees conflicts with confirmation-bias when branch probabilities are tuned to validate the analyst's preferred path.
expansions.why_relevant: Decision-trees conflicts with confirmation-bias when branch probabilities are tuned to validate the analyst's preferred path.

failure_hints.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Experimentation theater can produce dashboards and learning language without changing the underlying decision.
Vanity signals can be misread as validated learning even when they are disconnected from genuine adoption or fit.
Availability-style hammer use can lock the team into an MVP pattern that no longer fits the real problem.
Analysis paralysis can keep the team waiting for perfect information instead of learning through reversible movement.
Anchoring on the first option presented can quietly shrink the perceived option set and bias later evaluation.
Boundaries fail when they are drawn to protect comfort, politics, or convenience instead of the actual purpose of the work.
Premature narrowing can kill options and block the generation of better explanations or solutions.
A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that kills the favored branch.
Confirmation bias or Maslow's hammer can shape the tree around the analyst's preferred hypothesis instead of the real structure.

heuristic_hints.text: Keep the experiment tied to a clear hypothesis and gather only enough facts to prove or disprove it.
Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
Define the beachhead clearly so the team bites off a foothold before chasing the larger vision.
Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the current product hypothesis instead of boiling the ocean.
Bias toward action on reversible decisions because they buy information at low downside cost.
Invent first and decide later so evaluation does not choke off option generation too early.
Formalize the scope and expected output before the analysis sprawls.
Use boundaries to respect real cognitive limits by breaking work into genuinely manageable chunks.
Define branch-kill thresholds in advance so the team knows exactly which observed condition ends the favored path instead of improvising under pressure.
Embed explicit branch logic so the system can name when action A should beat action B.

premortem_hints.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
What do you think the other side doesn't get about your perspective?
What would you have to believe?
Which assumptions about demand, fit, or behavior would force a pivot rather than a continuation?
Is the worst case bad enough?
Have we generated enough options before narrowing the choice set?
What is this work actually supposed to cover, and what is deliberately out of scope?
Are these limits serving the goal, or are they mainly protecting us from difficult evidence or accountability?

Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?

select_when: Uncertainty can be reduced faster and cheaper through targeted tests than through longer debate, forecasting, or opinion escalation.
Each experiment can be tied to a clear hypothesis, success threshold, and downstream decision instead of being run for its own sake.
Reversible probes can inform larger irreversible commitments, reallocations, or product choices.
The team is stuck in argument about what is true, but a structured test could resolve the dispute.
danger_when: Experimentation has become theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision.
Local test wins are being overgeneralized while the team ignores system interactions, long-term effects, or behavior changes outside the test window.

select_when: A product or market hypothesis is still fragile enough that learning speed matters more than polished completeness.
The team can scope experiments narrowly, measure them honestly, and use the result to continue, pivot, or kill a path.
The work is at risk of wasting effort because assumptions about demand or fit have not been validated against real behavior.
A beachhead, foothold, or one-day-answer framing would improve focus compared with broad undifferentiated ambition.
danger_when: Weak usage blips or vanity metrics are being mislabeled as validated learning.
The domain has compliance, safety, or trust thresholds that cannot be responsibly approximated by a thin MVP shortcut.

select_when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call.
Decisions are reversible enough that preserving multiple paths creates information value rather than just indecision.
Downside can be capped while upside remains open across several plausible futures.
The problem has collapsed into binary framing, but the real move is to create more viable paths before choosing.
danger_when: Optionality has become a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit.
Maintaining optionality is consuming cash, attention, or organizational simplicity that would be better spent on a chosen path.

select_when: A project, analysis, or negotiation needs explicit scope lines before effort spreads into unowned terrain.
The current work is overloaded because limits on responsibility, attention, or working-memory burden have not been set clearly enough.
A team needs to distinguish what is in frame, out of frame, and who owns each part before deeper analysis becomes useful.
The next useful move is to define constraints that protect purpose rather than let complexity expand by default.
danger_when: Scope lines are being drawn mainly to avoid inconvenient evidence, hard conversations, or accountability.
The inquiry is being narrowed so early that important options, causes, or adjacent realities will be filtered out before they are understood.

select_when: A decision has explicit branches, trigger conditions, or kill criteria that can be made visible before resources are committed.
The branching logic has to be explicit before commitment because the wrong branch would create irreversible downside once a threshold, approval, or counterparty response lands.
Thresholds, approvals, or countermoves can be expressed clearly enough that the tree sharpens action rather than merely documenting options.
The next action depends on naming the branch that activates if one condition fails.
danger_when: Payoffs or probabilities are so unstable that a neat tree would create false precision and confidence rather than real guidance.
Strong feedback loops, path dependence, or interdependencies make the branches less independent than the diagram implies.

frame_elements.element_text: Solo founder assumes pivot signal from three unprompted customers vs pushing current flat product
frame_elements.evidence_quote: Solo founder assumes pivot signal from three unprompted customers vs pushing current flat product
frame_elements.fragility_signal: Three customer signals are conversational interest, not commercial commitment (e.g., no specific price/timeline), making non-pivot growth tactics like feature expansion or sales push equally viable

frame_elements.element_text: fixed: 18mo stage, $4K MRR, 22 customers, 14mo runway, two employees; excludes other pivots or non-pivot growth tactics
frame_elements.evidence_quote: fixed: 18mo stage, $4K MRR, 22 customers, 14mo runway, two employees; excludes other pivots or non-pivot growth tactics
frame_elements.fragility_signal: Runway and team size allow testing non-pivot tactics (e.g., customer expansion, pricing experiments) without immediate pivot, expanding options beyond binary pivot/no-pivot

reframings.reframed_question: What non-pivot growth tactics, like feature expansions or sales pushes on the current product, could the solo founder test with 14 months runway and two employees?
reframings.what_opens: Explores viability of expanding or selling the existing product using conversational customer signals as leads, rather than treating them solely as pivot indicators

reframings.reframed_question: How might the solo founder leverage the three unprompted customer signals to boost growth in the current flat product instead of pivoting to a new workflow tool?
reframings.what_opens: Opens path to interpreting weak signals as opportunities for current product iteration, challenging the binary pivot vs. push assumption

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Distinguishes sunk costs implicitly by focusing on future runway; identifies exit costs for current customers (freeze/migrate/sunset) and employee layoff with severance; recommends staged commitment via pre-buy test to preserve optionality before full pivot.
No modeling of competitor responses, retaliation, imitation, preemption, or first-mover dynamics in SaaS pivot; ignores how rivals might react to product shift.
dimensions.materiality_note: Competitors could copy the new workflow tool or target migrating customers, potentially reversing pivot viability

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Explicitly separates existing 22 customers (protect base via freeze/migrate/sunset, avoiding drag on pivot) from new workflow opportunity (validate via pre-buy); addresses cannibalization/distraction risks by planning transition.

Identifies CS employee's incentives (uncertainty, layoff vs pivot role) and realigns via advance notice/severance; former co-founder as passive shareholder (inform but no decision say); distinguishes principal-agent dynamics.
Assesses signal quality (conversational vs commercial, unprompted strength at low MRR); tests via pre-buy to distinguish noise from signal; sets pass bar to avoid bias.
Mentions runway/employees but does not identify displaced alternatives (e.g., growth tactics on current product vs pivot) or compare marginal returns across options like non-pivot optimization.
dimensions.materiality_note: Runway scarcity means pivot forgoes current MRR growth potential, could substantially alter if existing product has higher ROI

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Sizes downsides (layoff, customer churn, runway burn); distinguishes mitigate (pre-buy test, staged transition) vs absorb (honesty conversations); addresses recovery (severance, migration).
Maps stakeholders (customers: transition options; CS employee: notice/layoff; ex-founder: inform); influence strategy via hard conversations to build agreement/manage resistance.
Specifies sequence (pre-buy test this week → decision in 14 days → conversations/pivot); rationale for order (avoid indecision, enable data-first); critical path with deadlines.
Acknowledges conversational signal uncertainty but does not classify (epistemic reducible via test vs true Knightian); no calibration of confidence or matching strategy to type beyond test.
dimensions.materiality_note: Misclassifying demand uncertainty as fully reducible by 3 customers could lead to overconfident pivot, reversing recommendation

questions: Who are the main competitors offering similar workflow tools to those three customers right now?
What would those competitors most likely do if you successfully pivoted and captured those three customers?
Which of your 22 current customers might switch to a competitor if you freeze or migrate their access during a pivot?
What other uses could your engineer and CS employee's time serve besides building the new workflow tool over the next 14 months?
How would reallocating budget from the current product's maintenance to pivot development impact your 14-month runway?
What non-salary costs for your two employees, like tools or benefits, are you factoring into the pivot timeline?
What specific unknowns about those three customers' willingness to pre-buy make this feel like true uncertainty rather than measurable risk?
Beyond the pre-buy test, what ignorance gaps about market demand for the workflow tool could derail the pivot?
How does the flat growth over 4 months inform whether the current product's challenges are predictable risk or something unknowable?

triage_scores.evidence: Faint signal in recommending courtesy update to former co-founder due to 'friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends' and preventing 'multi-year relationship problem', which slightly prioritizes affection over pure decision efficiency.

Answer imposes tight 14-day decision deadline and pre-defined pass bar (2/3 yes = pivot) on a pivot decision amid 14-month runway, prematurely collapsing uncertainty by mandating closure despite only conversational signals and flat growth constraints.
Faint reference to runway shrinking without a decision date, but no adrenaline-fueled compression of normal scrutiny beyond setting a calendar date.

Answer recommends informing CS employee and former co-founder early 'before you've made the decision' and 'she deserves to hear this' due to her leaving a stable job and owning equity, treating passive shareholder courtesy as a material input to timing the pivot process despite query framing solo founder decision.

Overweights the 'three unprompted customers' as a 'real signal, not a weak one' and stronger than prompted at $50K MRR, treating it as decision-driving without referencing base rates from the 22 paying customers or denominators like flat growth over 4 months and $4K MRR scale.

Acknowledges founder's 'fear' of hard conversations as real but frames them as relieving for customers/employee ('honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them') and prioritizes them over indecision, potentially downplaying the emotional pain of layoffs and resentment to make action feel less painful.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Distinguish between conversational customer signals and commercial commitments to assess true pivot viability
evidence_quotes: "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR.
The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes establish the distinction between weak conversational interest and strong commercial intent, setting the foundation for requiring higher evidence before pivoting.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Propose a concrete pre-buy test with specific criteria to quickly validate the customer signal
evidence_quotes: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?"
If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.

write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes define the empirical test, pass/fail thresholds, and pre-commitment to criteria, providing a falsifiable method to upgrade the signal.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Outline structured transition options for existing customers to avoid pivot drag

query:
Solo founder decides whether to pivot B2B SaaS product from current offering to new workflow tool amid flat growth, $4K MRR, and 14 months runway.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] runway 14 months left
- [ACTIVE] MRR $4K, growth flat 4 months
- [ACTIVE] 22 paying customers
- [ACTIVE] two full-time employees (engineer, CS)
- [ACTIVE] three conversational customer signals, no price/timeline
- [ACTIVE] former co-founder 15% equity, inactive

Original framing: Solo founder assumes pivot signal from three unprompted customers vs pushing current flat product; fixed: 18mo stage, $4K MRR, 22 customers, 14mo runway, two employees; excludes other pivots or non-pivot growth tactics.
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Run pre-buy test this week with the three customers at specific price to validate signal (2/3 yes = pivot signal, all no = feature idea). Set decision in 14 days with pre-defined pass bar. If pivot: transition current 22 customers (freeze/migrate/sunset options); inform/talk to CS employee re uncertainty then likely layoff with severance; inform former co-founder without giving decision say. Prioritize hard conversations over strategic indecision to enable the decision.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
A few things worth separating. "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. But I want to ask: are those three customers in your existing customer base, or are they prospects you've been pitching? And has the conversation gone past "we'd pay more for that" into "here's what I'd pay, here's when, here's what it would replace"?

The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial. Those two signals look the same on a gut level but they're different enough to drive the decision.

---

OK — that changes the weight. Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade. Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI. If they won't do that, the signal you have is "people like the idea of the thing" which is qualitatively different from "people will pay for the thing."

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.

---

Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate; or sell the customer list to a competitor, which is rare at your scale but not impossible in vertical SaaS.

The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot. You'll keep doing bug fixes and small features "just for them" and the pivot will take 50% longer than it should. Be honest with them early about what's happening and give them a clean option.

On your fear — it's real, and it's also the thing that often kills pivots. Founders delay the hard customer conversation because they're protecting their own hope. The customers know something is wrong when growth is flat for four months. Your honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them.

---

You have three options and none of them are painless.

First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role. This is the most common answer and often the best one. You don't drag her through a project where she has nothing to do.

Second: pivot her role. Is there something she could do during the build period — market research, early pipeline development for the new product, partnership conversations? If the work is real and useful, this can work. If you're inventing busywork, she'll see through it immediately and it's worse than the layoff.

Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do. If you decide to look around, I'll support that and I'll still give you severance when the pivot happens." Some people appreciate the advance notice; others will leave immediately, which may or may not suit you.

My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms. She deserves to hear this before you've made the decision, not after. The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming.

---

She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.

Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it." You're not asking for permission; you're keeping her in the loop as someone who still owns part of the company.

This matters less for the pivot decision itself and more for your relationship with her. Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.

---

One thing. Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot" while your runway shrinks and your employees wonder what's happening.

Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call. Not "we'll see how it feels" — an actual date on your calendar where you decide. The decision is easier when you've pre-committed to making it.

Also worth adding: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.

---

Of course. One last thing. The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.

Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them. Good luck.
